content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 10, 2018

Donna Mooney
Chief Assistant City Attorney
City Attorney's Office
555 Santa Clara Street
Vallejo, CA 94590

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-067

Dear Ms. Mooney:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Councilmember Robert
McConnell regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and
not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or
Section 1090. The Commission does not provide advice relating to past conduct and any
conclusions contained herein apply only to prospective actions. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).)

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

QUESTION

May Councilmember McConnell participate in zoning code and ordinance amendments to
allow recreational marijuana business use in areas currently permitted for medical marijuana,
subject to the same conditions and operating requirements, where he has an interest in a multi-
family residential unit within 500 feet of an area subject to these decisions?

CONCLUSION

Yes. The facts do not indicate that these decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable and
material impact on his financial interest.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-067
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTOR

On May 22, 2018, the Vallejo City Council will consider whether to amend the zoning code to allow recreational marijuana (cannabis) cultivation, nonvolatile manufacturing and distribution in areas with the following zoning designations: Intensive Use, Intensive Use-Limited, and Planned Development Industrial.

Medical marijuana activity of the same kind is presently allowed in those three zones. The zoning code amendment includes operating requirements such as adequate odor absorption and ventilating systems, which are also required for medical marijuana businesses. Only 11 businesses total will be allowed to conduct marijuana activity in the zones, and are limited to no more than two sites per business.

On May 22, 2018, the Council will also consider an amendment to a regulatory ordinance that will require recreational marijuana businesses, like medical marijuana businesses, to have, among other things, a security plan and theft prevention plan to minimize crime.

Councilmember McConnell owns a multi-family residential unit, in a low-density residential zone, which City staff estimates is less than 500 feet, and less than one block, from an area zoned as Intensive Use. In total, he owns 26 rental units in the City of Vallejo.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in the making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on one or more of the official's interests identified in that section. As applicable to the facts provide, Section 87103 identifies the following interests:

• Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)), or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

• Any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).)

• Any source of income aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the official within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

For the decision at issue, Councilmember McConnell has interests in his rental business as a business entity and source of income, the tenants of his rental business as sources of income, and the rental unit as a real property interest.

File No. A-18-067
Page No. 3

Foreseeability and Materiality

A decision's financial effect on an official's interest is presumed reasonably foreseeable if
the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) An interest is
the "subject" of a proceeding if the decision involves any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with an interest, including a decision affecting real property under Regulation
18702.2(a)(1)-(6). From the facts provided, Councilmember McConnell's interests are not a named
party in, or the subject of these decisions.

Where the interest is not a named party in or the subject of the decision, as we have here, the
decision's financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as "a realistic possibility
and more than hypothetical or theoretical." (Regulation 18701(b).) Looking to the issue of
materiality, the following standards are applicable to his financial interests:

Real Property Interest: Residential Rental Property

Regulation 18702.2(a)(7) through (12) sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a
decision's financial effect on an official's interest in real property that is not explicitly involved in
the decision. In pertinent part, a financial effect is material if the decision:

10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially
altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property
surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or
air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market
value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial interest;

(11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located within 500
feet of the perp line of the official's real property other than commercial
property containing a business entity where the materiality standards are analyzed
under Regulation 18702.1. Notwithstanding this prohibition, the Commission may
provide written advice allowing an official to participate under these
circumstances if the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to
indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the
official's property;

Business Interest: Rental Property Business

Regulation 18702.1(b) sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a decision's financial
effect on an official's interest in a business entity, including one arising from a source of income,²
that is not explicitly involved in the decision. Under Regulation 18702.1(b), a financial effect is
material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it reasonably foreseeable that the
effect would contribute to a change in the value of the business entity.

² See Regulation 18702.3(a)(4).

File No. A-18-067
Page No. 4

Regulation 18702.1(b) provides the following types of decisions that may have a material
effect on a business entity:

(1) Authorize, prohibit, regulate or otherwise establish conditions for an activity
in which the business entity is engaged;

(2) Increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which the
business entity is engaged;

(3) Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the business
entity supplies;

(4) Make improvements in the surrounding neighborhood such as redevelopment
projects, traffic/road improvements, or parking changes that may affect, either
temporarily or permanently, the amount of business the business entity receives;

(5) Decide the location of a major development, entertainment facility, or other
project that would increase or decrease the amount of business the entity draws
from the location of the project; or

(6) Increase or decrease the tax burden, debt, or financial or legal liability of the
business entity.

No facts are provided which implicate the above factors. There is no indication that
Councilmember McConnell's residential rental property will face altered levels of intensity of use,
above and beyond the current conditions, as a result of the decisions. The residential rental property
is within 500 feet of the area zoned as Intensive Use subject to the amendments and ordinance.
However, based on the fact that this Intensive Use area is already zoned to allow medical marijuana
cultivation, manufacturing and distribution businesses and with no facts to the contrary, the
extension of permissible use to include recreational marijuana does not appear to present a
reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's property.

Similarly, the facts do not implicate any of the factors regarding changes to his rental
business, set forth above. Therefore, Councilmember McConnell does not have a conflict of interest
and may participate in the decision.³

3 The facts provided do not indicate a foreseeable and material effect on the tenants of the rental property
resulting from an effect on their rental unit. However, if Councilmember McConnell knows or has reason to know that a
tenant has an interest in any other property or business implicated by the decision, the Councilmember may be
disqualified from the decision and should seek further advice. (See Regulation 18702.3.)

File No. A-18-067
Page No. 5

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

By: L. Karen Harrison
Counsel, Legal Division

LKH:jgl
